item.title: Enriched Category
item.description:
An enriched category over a monoidal category V is a category-like structure whose hom-objects are objects of V rather than sets. An ordinary category is enriched over Set (with Cartesian product); replacing Set with a richer base V allows the morphisms between two objects to carry additional structure — topological, algebraic, or homotopical.

An enriched category C consists of: a collection of objects, for each pair (A, B) a hom-object Hom(A, B) in V, composition maps Hom(B, C) ⊗ Hom(A, B) → Hom(A, C) using the tensor product of V, and identity maps I → Hom(A, A) from the unit object. These must satisfy associativity and unit laws expressed as commutative diagrams in V.

item.title: Hom-Object
item.description:
The hom-object Hom(A, B) in an enriched category over V is the object in the monoidal category V that plays the role of “morphisms from A to B.” In an ordinary category, hom-objects are sets (the hom-sets); in an enriched category, they can be topological spaces, chain complexes, simplicial sets, or any other type of object in V.

Composition in an enriched category is a map Hom(B, C) ⊗ Hom(A, B) → Hom(A, C) using the tensor product of V, and identity morphisms are maps I → Hom(A, A) from the unit object. The structure on hom-objects is what makes enrichment meaningful: in a topologically enriched category, composition is continuous; in a simplicially enriched category, morphisms form a space whose points are morphisms and whose higher simplices witness homotopies between them.

item.title: Monoidal Category
item.description:
A monoidal category is a category C equipped with a tensor product ⊗: C × C → C, a unit object I, and coherence isomorphisms for associativity ((A ⊗ B) ⊗ C ≅ A ⊗ (B ⊗ C)) and units (I ⊗ A ≅ A ≅ A ⊗ I). These isomorphisms must satisfy the pentagon and triangle coherence conditions, ensuring that all ways of re-associating and inserting units agree.

A monoidal category is the categorical generalization of a monoid: where a monoid has elements combined by an operation with an identity, a monoidal category has objects combined by a tensor product with a unit object. A monoid in the category of sets is an ordinary monoid; a monoid in a monoidal category is a monoid object — an object M with multiplication M ⊗ M → M and unit I → M satisfying the monoid axioms.

item.title: Tensor Product
item.description:
The tensor product in a monoidal category is a bifunctor ⊗: C × C → C that combines two objects into one. It is the categorical analogue of multiplication: just as a monoid has a binary operation on elements, a monoidal category has a binary operation on objects.

The tensor product must be associative up to coherent isomorphism: (A ⊗ B) ⊗ C ≅ A ⊗ (B ⊗ C). It must have a unit object I with I ⊗ A ≅ A ≅ A ⊗ I. The tensor product need not be commutative (A ⊗ B need not be isomorphic to B ⊗ A); when it is, the monoidal category is called symmetric.

item.title: Unit Object
item.description:
The unit object I in a monoidal category is the object that acts as an identity for the tensor product: I ⊗ A ≅ A ≅ A ⊗ I for every object A, via coherent isomorphisms. It is the categorical analogue of the identity element in a monoid.

In the category of sets with Cartesian product, the unit object is any singleton set {∗}. In the category of vector spaces with tensor product, the unit is the base field. In the category of abelian groups with tensor product over ℤ, the unit is ℤ itself.
